content.text: The fastest way to run React code is to load it from a CDN inside an HTML file — no npm, no config, no terminal. You write JSX in a

tag and the browser compiles it on the fly using Babel Standalone. This approach is deliberately limited and should not be used for production. Its purpose is to let you focus on React concepts without tooling noise, share a tiny reproducible example, or drop a small React widget into an existing HTML page.

attrs.alt: CDN setup vs build-tool setup comparison: CDN loads React and Babel from unpkg, compiles JSX in the browser, limited to dev use; build tools enable npm packages, TypeScript, and optimised production bundles
attrs.title: CDN setup vs build tool setup

content.text: Three script tags are required: React itself, ReactDOM (the browser renderer), and Babel Standalone for JSX. Copy this into a file, double-click to open in a browser, and you have a working React app:
are accessed as global variables — the UMD builds attach themselves to
. In a project with a bundler you would

tag and transforms the JSX to plain JavaScript before the browser's JS engine runs it. The compilation happens on every page load, which is why this is only suitable for development and prototyping — Babel Standalone alone weighs about 800 KB, and parsing happens synchronously on the main thread.
You can also skip JSX entirely and call
directly. This removes the need for Babel:
Using htm — JSX Without a Compiler

(Hyperscript Markup Templates) is a tiny library by Jason Miller that uses tagged template literals to provide JSX-like syntax without any compilation step. You get readable JSX-style code without loading Babel:
— this unlocks native ES module imports so you can pull packages directly from a CDN like esm.sh without a bundler.
ES Module Imports from a CDN
Modern browsers support native ES modules. You can import npm packages directly from CDNs that serve them as ES modules — no bundler required:
Converts any npm package to ESM; handles sub-paths
Reliable, fast, UMD and ESM formats

<script type="module">
  import React, { useState } from 'https://esm.sh/react@18';
  import { createRoot } from 'https://esm.sh/react-dom@18/client';

  function Counter() {
    const [n, setN] = useState(0);
    return React.createElement(
      'button',
      { onClick: () => setN(n + 1) },
      `Clicked ${n} times`
    );
  }

  createRoot(document.getElementById('root')).render(
    React.createElement(Counter)
  );
</script>
Adding React to an Existing HTML Page
You can render a React component into a single
inside an otherwise non-React page. This is the original "add React gradually" use case:

Welcome to My Website

This is plain HTML.

More static HTML below.

React ships both development and production UMD bundles. The development build includes warnings and stack traces; the production build is minified and omits them. Always pin a specific version so the page does not break when React publishes a new major:

<!-- Development (large, verbose errors) -->
<script src="https://unpkg.com/react@18.3.1/umd/react.development.js" crossorigin></script>
<script src="https://unpkg.com/react-dom@18.3.1/umd/react-dom.development.js" crossorigin></script>

<!-- Production (minified, no dev warnings) -->
<script src="https://unpkg.com/react@18.3.1/umd/react.production.min.js" crossorigin></script>
<script src="https://unpkg.com/react-dom@18.3.1/umd/react-dom.production.min.js" crossorigin></script>

Even in production UMD mode you still cannot use Babel Standalone — that is only for development. For production you must pre-compile JSX with a build tool. If you need JSX-like syntax without a build step, use
An import map lets you write bare specifiers (
) in native ES module scripts without a bundler, by mapping the specifier to a URL. Chrome 89+, Firefox 108+, and Safari 16.4+ all support them:
Cannot use React Router, Zustand, date-fns, etc.
Babel Standalone can strip types with @babel/preset-typescript, but no type-checking
Full page reload required on every save
All scripts load up front; no lazy loading
Babel Standalone (~800 KB) must download and parse every load

For prototyping and sharing snippets, online sandboxes give you the CDN convenience with the dev-experience of a real build tool. They are the practical middle ground between a local project and a bare HTML file:
Full Vite environment, npm installs, TypeScript
Runs Vite in WebContainers (browser Node.js), fast
CDN-style, no install step, Babel preset enabled
When a CDN prototype grows into something real, the migration to Vite takes about five minutes:
Copy your component code from the
etc. — the UMD globals no longer exist.
It adds ~800 KB to the page and compiles JSX on the main thread, blocking first render. Only use it for prototyping and local experiments.
on script tags loaded from unpkg, React's error overlay cannot show full component stack traces.
means a CDN update can change the behaviour of your page without you making any change. Pin exact versions in production.
with an ESM import of the same package, you get two separate React instances. All hooks must use the same React instance — mixing them causes
does not exist in the DOM when the script runs,
throws. Either place the script at the bottom of

Because there is no build step, debugging is straightforward: the file you write is the file the browser runs (after Babel's in-browser pass). However, error messages can be cryptic when Babel compiles JSX incorrectly. A few tips:
The Console and Sources panels work as normal. Source maps from Babel Standalone let the Sources panel show the original JSX line numbers.
prints component names in error messages and warns about common mistakes (e.g. a missing key prop). Switch to the production build only once the app is working.
The React DevTools browser extension works with CDN-loaded React just as well as with bundled apps. Install it from the Chrome or Firefox extension store to inspect the component tree and props.
If any CDN script fails to load (network error, typo in URL), the browser falls back silently and subsequent code throws with "React is not defined". Confirm all three scripts loaded successfully.
Even without a module system you can split code across multiple files and load them in order:

<!-- index.html -->
<!-- React scripts first -->
<script src="https://unpkg.com/react@18/umd/react.development.js" crossorigin></script>
<script src="https://unpkg.com/react-dom@18/umd/react-dom.development.js" crossorigin></script>
<script src="https://unpkg.com/@babel/standalone/babel.min.js"></script>

<!-- Your components — must be in dependency order -->
<script type="text/babel" src="components/Button.jsx"></script>
<script type="text/babel" src="components/Header.jsx"></script>
<script type="text/babel" src="App.jsx"></script>
<script type="text/babel" src="index.jsx"></script>
tag. Each file is fetched and compiled separately. Scripts must be in dependency order since there is no module graph to resolve imports automatically.
React 18 changed the root API. If you see old tutorials using
, that is the React 17 API and it shows a deprecation warning in React 18. Use
Babel Standalone can strip TypeScript types if you enable the
preset. Note that this only strips types — it does not type-check:

This is occasionally useful for quick experiments with TypeScript syntax, but since there is no type-checking, you get none of TypeScript's actual benefits. Use a build tool if you want real type safety.
Using Third-Party Packages from a CDN
Most popular React packages also ship UMD builds you can load from a CDN. Check the package's npm page or unpkg browser to find the UMD file. Load it after React and it attaches to

Not every package has a UMD build. Large libraries like MUI or React Router have complex dependency graphs and are much easier to consume through a bundler and npm. For anything beyond utility libraries, move to a build tool.
State Management Without a Build Tool
With only CDN scripts available, you are limited to React's built-in state primitives. The three most useful for small CDN apps are useState, useReducer, and Context:
If you need to work offline or want to avoid CDN latency, download the scripts once and serve them locally. Use any static file server:

# Download the scripts (one-time)
curl -o react.dev.js       https://unpkg.com/react@18/umd/react.development.js
curl -o react-dom.dev.js   https://unpkg.com/react-dom@18/umd/react-dom.development.js
curl -o babel.min.js       https://unpkg.com/@babel/standalone/babel.min.js

# Serve the directory with any static server
npx serve .         # or: python3 -m http.server 8080
Then update the script tags to use relative paths:
Opening a local HTML file directly via
URL can cause CORS errors when Babel tries to fetch external scripts or when ES modules are involved. Always use a local server (
or similar) rather than opening the file directly.
A CDN setup loads React, ReactDOM, and Babel Standalone via
tags; JSX compiles in the browser at runtime.
htm + native ES modules lets you write JSX-like syntax from a
Never use Babel Standalone in production — it is slow, large, and only meant for prototyping.
Online sandboxes (CodeSandbox, StackBlitz, CodePen) are the practical alternative to a local project for experimentation.
When the prototype grows into a real app, migrate to Vite in five steps: scaffold, copy component code, swap global React for named imports, install, run.
